problem_13.natural_language: If `f` and `g` commute, then `ker(f)` and `range(f)` are invariant under `g`.
problem_13.lean:
theorem ker_range_invtSubmodule_of_commute
    {K V : Type*} [CommSemiring K] [AddCommMonoid V] [Module K V]
    (f g : Module.End K V)
    (hfg : f * g = g * f) :
    LinearMap.ker f ∈ g.invtSubmodule ∧ LinearMap.range f ∈ g.invtSubmodule
problem_13.natural_language_proof:
Proof: assume `f g = g f`. For the kernel, let `x ∈ ker f`, so `f x = 0`. Then
`f (g x) = (f g) x = (g f) x = g (f x) = 0`, hence `g x ∈ ker f`.
For the range, take `y ∈ range f`, so `y = f x` for some `x`. Then
`g y = g (f x) = (g f) x = (f g) x = f (g x)`, which lies again in `range f`.
problem_13.lean_proof:
by
  constructor
  · intro y hy
    rw [Submodule.mem_comap]
    rw [LinearMap.ker]
    rw [Submodule.mem_comap]
    have hy' : f y = 0 := hy
    have h : f (g y) = g (f y) := congr_arg (fun h => h y) hfg
    rw [h, hy']
    exact (LinearMap.map_zero g)
  · intro y hy
    rw [Submodule.mem_comap]
    have hy' : ∃ x, f x = y := hy
    rcases hy' with ⟨x, rfl⟩
    have h : f (g x) = g (f x) := congr_arg (fun h => h x) hfg
    rw [← h]
    exact (LinearMap.mem_range_self f (g x))
